Notice Of Motion And Motion To Extend Time To Pay Monetary Sanctions And To Temporarily Stay Enforcement
Off Calendar Nov-21-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF GENOA MARTELL's Motion To Extend Time To Pay Monetary Sanctions And To Temporarily Stay Enforcement.

Plaintiff Genoa Martell's Motion To Extend Time To Pay Monetary Sanctions And To Temporarily Stay Enforcement is GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART.

The motion to stay enforcement of the sanctions is denied.

Good cause appearing the court modifies its September 22, 2025 Order to provide that Plaintiff must pay the sanctions ordered ($2,860) to Defendants in monthly installments of $476 due the first of each month, starting December 1, 2025, and ending May 1. 2026, except that the May 2026 payment shall be in the amount of $480 (the balance then remaining). If a payment is ten calendar days late, the full balance shall become due immediately.

Plaintiff's "limitations" showing was not compelling, especially given her level of activity in this case. Should Plaintiff engage in further activity that gives rise to sanctions, the court will expect payment under the terms set out in the court's sanctions order.

Order is signed.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/JMQ)

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

PLAINTIFF GENOA MARTELL's Motion To Compel Initial Responses To Plaintiff'S Requests For Production, Set Three (Cpp 2031.300).

Plaintiff Genoa Martell's Motion to Compel Initial Responses to Plaintiff's Requests for Production, Set Three is GRANTED.

The discovery requests at issue appear reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. (See Code of Civil Procedure section 2017.010.) The record demonstrates Defendant did not provide any responses to the RFPs. No meet and confer requirement exists for motions to compel responses for requests for production of documents when no responses have been provided. (Code of Civil Procedure section 2031.300.)

Plaintiff electronically served the RFPs on Defendant Wish US Holdings, Inc. on August 22, 2025. On September 26, 2025, Defendants did not ask for an extension due to the transition between Defendant's counsel. Instead, they told Plaintiff in an e-mail that they would provide responses within a couple weeks. (Martell Decl., Ex. B.) More than a month later, Defendants still had not provided any responses to Plaintiff's requests.

While it appears Defendant has now served responses and, therefore, no order compelling responses is necessary, it remains true that Defendant misused discovery and breached its obligations under the Discovery Act.

The court does not find Defendant Wish US Holdings, Inc. acted with substantial justification, nor does it find that imposition of sanctions Defendant Wish US Holdings, Inc. would otherwise be unjust. (See Code of Civil Procedure section 2031.300(c).) Defendant Wish US Holdings, Inc. shall pay $750 as sanctions, payable to the Clerk of the Court, payment no later than November 14, 2025, receipt filed no later than November 21, 2025. Such payment reimburses the court for a portion of the costs spent adjudicating this motion.

Off Calendar Oct-29-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

1 - PLAINTIFF GENOA MARTELL's Motion To Deem Requests For Admission (Set Three) Admitted.

Plaintiff's motion to deem her Requests for Admission in Set Three admitted is DENIED.

Plaintiff's request to deem her RFAs admitted is denied. Defendant's responses to Plaintiff's Requests for Admission, Set Three were due on September 24, 2025, yet Defendant did not respond until October 8, 2025. If a party fails to timely respond to requests for admission, the party waives any objections to the requests and the truth of any matters specified in the requests may be deemed admitted. (CCP 2033.280(a)-(b).) However, the court does not deem requests for admission admitted if "it finds that the party to whom the requests for admission have been directed has served, before the hearing on the motion, a proposed response to the requests for admission that is in substantial compliance with Section 2033.220." (CCP 2033.280(c).)

Here, there is no dispute that Defendants did not timely respond to Plaintiff's requests for admission. However, the court finds that Defendants have properly served responses prior to the hearing, which are in substantial compliance with Section 2033.220. Plaintiff contends that to be in substantial compliance, statements of inability to respond must identify who was consulted, what repositories were searched, which vendors were contacted, and when the inquiry occurred. Section 2033.220 does not require that level of specificity, and the court finds that Defendant's responses are in substantial compliance.

As for sanctions, the circumstances before the court would make an award of sanctions unjust. Additionally, Plaintiff failed to give timely and proper notice of the amount sought. The court would award Plaintiff the filing fee, but she has a fee waiver.

Order is signed.

MOTION TO STRIKE Amended ANSWER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-02-2025 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances in open Court. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

3 - PLAINTIFF GENOA MARTELL'S MOTION TO STRIKE Amended ANSWER to Amended COMPLAINT.

Plaintiff's motion to strike the amended answer is OFF CALENDAR.

A party moving to strike a pleading or a part of a pleading must meet and confer with the party who filed the pleading "[b]efore filing [the] motion." (Code of Civil Procedure section 435.5(a).) The meeting must be "in person, by telephone or by video conference." (Ibid.) The meeting must take place at least five days before the date the motion must be filed. (Code of Civil Procedure section 435.5(a)(2.) The moving party must file a declaration with its moving papers demonstrating compliance with these requirements and/or that the opposing party failed to confer in good faith. (Code of Civil Procedure section 435.5(3).)

Here, Plaintiff failed to demonstrate she met her meet and confer obligation. On 8/25/25, Defendant filed its answer. On 8/26/25, Plaintiff sent a meet and confer letter, and she admits that no substantive meet and confer occurred. (Martell Decl., par. 4.) Despite the failure to meet and confer, Plaintiff filed this motion on 8/29/25. Moreover, on 8/29/25, Defendant filed an amended answer and there was no meet and confer regarding that operative pleading. Section 435.5(a) is clear: "If an amended pleading is filed, the responding party shall meet and confer again with the party who filed the amended pleading before filing a motion to strike the amended pleading." Plaintiff's reply notes subsequent written communications between the parties after Plaintiff filed the motion. Those meet and confer efforts are insufficient. Section 435.5(a) requires parties to meet and confer "[b]efore filing a motion to strike" and the parties must do so "in person, by telephone, or by video conference." The parties are ordered to comply with the code.

Order is signed.

Motion To Compel Further Responses To First Discovery Requests To Defendant Wish Us Holdings, Llc
Off Calendar Aug-11-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

1 - PLAINTIFF GENOA MARTELL' Motion To Compel Further Responses To First Discovery Requests To Defendant Wish Us Holdings, Llc.

Plaintiff Genoa Martell's motion to compel further responses is DENIED as untimely.

Plaintiff seeks to compel Defendant Wish US Holdings, LLC's further responses to her first set of requests for production of documents, requests for admission, special interrogatories, and form interrogatories, propounded on February 9, 2025.

Defendant served initial responses to these requests on March 13, 2025, and supplemented some of these responses on April 29, 2025. (Martell decl. para. 2-3, 6.) Under Code of Civil Procedure sections 2031.310 and 1010.6, Plaintiff had to file this motion at the latest by Tuesday, June 17, 2025 (45 days from April 29th plus two court days for e-service). Defendant did not agree to extend the deadline past June 17, 2025. Plaintiff filed this motion on June 23, 2025, several days past this deadline.

Plaintiff argues the court should exercise its discretion to consider this motion for good cause. She explains she was waiting to file this motion in good faith, and out of judicial economy, to see if Defendant would cure the alleged deficiencies. This deadline is mandatory and jurisdictional, and the "the court [is] without authority to rule on motions to compel other than to deny them." (See Sexton v. Superior Court (1996) 58 Cal.App.4th 1403, 1410.)

Plaintiff also argues that Defendant created procedural ambiguities by offering to extend her initial deadline to May 19, 2025, prior to supplementing its responses. (Mot. 5:7-8.) This argument is irrelevant because Plaintiff did not move to compel by May 19, 2025. The 45-day deadline ran from the date of Defendant's 4/29/25 supplemental responses, and Defendant did not agree to extend this deadline further. Moreover, procedural confusion is not grounds to extend this deadline under Sexton.

Plaintiff did not act with substantial justification in connection with this discovery and motion. Plaintiff shall pay $560 as sanctions to Defendant Wish US Holdings by August 29, 2025.

Order is signed.

Motion For Terminating Sanctions To Wish Us Holdings Llc
Off Calendar May-15-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

1 - PLAINTIFF GENOA MARTELL's Motion For Terminating Sanctions To Wish Us Holdings LLLC.

Plaintiff's motion for terminating sanctions and entry of default against defendant Wish US Holdings LLC is DENIED.

Plaintiff has not demonstrated that she satisfied the meet and confer obligation, that is the obligation to make reasonable and good efforts to informally resolve the discovery dispute before presenting it to the court. On April 8, 2025, plaintiff sent a purported meet and confer letter with attachments to defendant that identified numerous alleged deficiencies in defendant's discovery responses. (Martell Decl., Ex. 3.) The letter arbitrarily imposed a deadline of April 14, 2025, to cure each deficiency. In response, by e-mail dated April 10, 2025, defense counsel noted the unfairness of requiring amended responses in less than a week, stated that he would attempt to respond to the issues by April 17, 2025, and extended the deadline for plaintiff to bring a motion to May 19, 2025. (Martell Decl., Ex. 3A.) In fact, on April 17, 2025, defendant did respond to plaintiff and agreed to provide some amended responses. (Wahl Decl., Exs. A-D.) On April 29, 2025, defendant served supplemental responses. (Wahl Decl., par. 7.)

Rather than engage in the discovery process in good faith, plaintiff rushed to court on April 15, 2025, and filed the instant motion-precisely the conduct the legislature prohibited when it imposed the meet-and-confer duty. Plaintiff's attempt to employ the "nuclear option" while defendant was attempting to respond to plaintiff's concerns is the antithesis of meeting and conferring in good faith. The motion is therefore denied. (CCP 2016.040 ["A meet and confer declaration in support of a motion shall state facts showing a reasonable and good faith attempt at an informal resolution of each issue presented by the motion."]; Clement v. Alegre (2009) 177 Cal.App.4th 1277, 1294 ["[T]he statute requires that there be a serious effort at negotiation and informal resolution."].)

Order is signed.

DEFENDANT WISH US HOLDINGS LLC DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT.

Defendant Wish US Holdings LLC's demurrer is OFF CALENDAR.

On February 13, 2025, the court took Defendant's earlier demurrer off calendar and granted Plaintiff Genoa Martell leave to file a second amended complaint on or before March 7, 2025. Importantly, at that time the court ordered that subsequent meet and confer regarding demurrers must be by "remote platform such as Zoom" and "recorded." This directive was to avoid the incessant finger pointing that has come to characterize this case. For this demurrer, Defense counsel testifies the meet and confer occurred during "a phone call conversation." (Wahl Dec. para. 5. ) Defendant failed to follow the court's order and, therefore, the demurrer is off calendar.

In its directive, the court contemplated that the next demurrer would be to the second amended complaint, but that never got filed. The court's intent, though, was clear: subsequent Code-required meet and confer must be by remote video platform such as Zoom and recorded by the parties.

On its own motion, the court grants Plaintiff leave to file her second amended complaint no later than May 23, 2025. No extensions of time as to this order are contemplated.

All Code-required meet and confer must be conducted by remote video platform such as Zoom and recorded by the parties. Going forward, the parties are ordered to make a concerted effort to cooperate to bring this case to resolution. "It is the policy of the state that a plaintiff shall proceed with reasonable diligence in the prosecution of an action but that all parties shall cooperate in bringing the action to trial or other disposition." (Code of Civil Procedure section 583.130.) "The policy of the state is that the parties to a lawsuit 'shall cooperate.' Period. Full stop." (Lasalle v. Vogel (2019) 36 Cal.App.5th 127, 130.)

Amended Notice Of Motion To Compel Further And/Or Amended Responses To Request For Production, Set One
Off Calendar Feb-07-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: RE: PLAINTIFF GENOA MARTELL'S, AN INDIVIDUAL Amended Notice Of Motion To Compel Further And/Or Amended Responses To Request For Production, Set One.

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Al West, Esq., Law Offices: West & Associates, (310) 372-4137, Limited Representation Attorney for and with Genoa Martell, Plaintiff.
Joseph M. Wahl, Esq., of Barnes & Thornburg LLP, for Defendants Wish US Holdings, LLC; and Qoo10 Pte. Ltd.

The matter is argued. At the conclusion of argument, the court adopts its tentative ruling. Plaintiff alleges that her former employer ContextLogic, Inc. dba Wish (hereinafter "Wish") misreported her income and caused adverse tax consequences. (First Amended Complaint ("FAC"), pars. 18-27.) Approximately four years later, defendant Qoo10 Pte. Ltd purchased some of Wish's assets and liabilities. (FAC, par. 12.) Plaintiff propounded this request for production on Qoo10 Pte. Ltd. ("Quoo10"). In response, Qoo10 stated, inter alia, that "Responding Party has made a diligent search and reasonable inquiry in an effort to locate the requested item. Responding Party is unable to comply with this Request because such documents are not in Responding Party's possession, custody, or control. Investigation and discovery are continuing and Responding Party reserves the right to supplement this response."

Plaintiff's supporting declaration for this motion does not attach the request for production nor the response. Rather, plaintiff attached the requests for admission and Qoo10's response. Nevertheless, based on the parties' separate statements and arguments presented, the court infers that defendant's response to the request for production was properly verified. Defendant relies on CCP 2031.230 to argue that it cannot respond. In such a situation, "[t]he statement shall set forth the name and address of any natural person or organization known or believed by that party to have possession, custody, or control of that item or category of item." (CCP Section 2031.230.) Defendant has not set forth that information and given Qoo10's relationship with Wish, the answer is evasive.

Plaintiff's meet and confer was reasonable. The requests at issue are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. (CCP Section 2017.010.) Plaintiff has shown Qoo10's responses are incomplete. Qoo10 has not demonstrated that any objection has merit. The court orders Qoo10 to provide verified responses. For the responses to which Qoo10 is claiming a privilege, Defendant shall produce a privilege log. The responses and privilege log shall be provided no later than 10 court days from notice of this order.

The court denies sanctions since both sides acted with substantial justification.

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Al West, Esq., Law Offices: West & Associates, (310) 372-4137, Limited Representation Attorney for and with Genoa Martell, Plaintiff.
Joseph M. Wahl, Esq., of Barnes & Thornburg LLP, for Defendants Wish US Holdings, LLC; and Qoo10 Pte. Ltd.

The matter is argued. At the conclusion of argument, the court adopts its tentative ruling. Plaintiff alleges that her former employer ContextLogic, Inc. dba Wish (hereinafter "Wish") misreported her income and caused adverse tax consequences. (First Amended Complaint ("FAC"), pars. 18-27.) Approximately four years later, defendant Qoo10 Pte. Ltd purchased some of Wish's assets and liabilities. (FAC, par. 12.)

Plaintiff served special interrogatories on Qoo10 Pte. Ltd. ("Quoo10"). In response, Qoo10 stated, inter alia, that it had no information responsive to the interrogatories.
Plaintiff's supporting declaration for this motion does not attach the special interrogatories or responses. Rather, plaintiff attached the requests for admission and Qoo10's response. Nevertheless, based on the parties' separate statements and arguments presented, the court infers that defendant's response to the special interrogatories was properly verified. Defendant relies on CCP 2030.220(c) to contend that it cannot respond. But defendant was obligated to "make a reasonable and good faith effort to obtain the information by inquiry to other natural persons or organizations." The court concludes that defendant's responses were evasive. For example, special interrogatory 3 asks "DESCRIBE with specificity YOUR relationship with ContextLogic Inc., a/k/a Wish." Somebody in defendant's organization should have information regarding this issue as defendant concedes that it acquired some of Wish's liabilities and assets. Plaintiff's meet and confer was reasonable. The interrogatories at issue are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. (CCP Section 2017.010.) Plaintiff has shown Qoo10's responses are incomplete. Qoo10 has not demonstrated that any objection has merit. The court orders Qoo10 to provide verified responses. For the responses to which Qoo10 is claiming a privilege, Defendant shall produce a privilege log. The responses and privilege log shall be provided no later than 10 court days from notice of this order. The court cannot find that defendant acted with substantial justification. Defendants and counsel, jointly and severally, shall pay $1,100 to plaintiff in sanctions, payment no later than 10 court days from notice of this order.
